item.title: Tariff Refunds on Hold — What Happens Now?
item.description: An upcoming Washington Post Opinions show. Stay tuned.

item.title: Paying tribute requires respect
item.description: International law enforcement, affordability, plastics and more, in readers eyes.

item.title: Putin’s actions in Iran demand a U.S. response
item.description: Russia is counting on Iran distracting the United States from Ukraine.

item.title: Utah lawmakers want to protect kids. An illegal new tax won’t help.
item.description: Small businesses will suffer from the levy on targeted internet ads.

item.title: Schools are teaching AI — and making a massive mistake
item.description: They should teach kids to have agency over the technology, not just how to use it.

item.title: Mortgage rates just fell below a crucial psychological threshold
item.description: The difference from a 6 percent rate is tiny, but the appeal to customers is big.

item.title: Look who is wrong about the economy now
item.description: Americans called it: This economy is not what it seems.

item.title: A new way of identifying ultra-processed food can clarify food choices
item.description: Identifying unhealthy food is not as easy as one might think.

item.title: Why are Americans ‘Chinamaxxing’? Look in the mirror.
item.description: The popular social media trend doesn’t mean China is winning the soft power war.

item.title: This ‘no-brainer’ tax credit is splitting governors down party lines
item.description: Can Colorado Gov. Jared Polis lead the way for fellow Democrats?

item.title: The right and wrong ways to reduce oil prices
item.description: America could be spared the worst, but the world will suffer.

item.title: Sky high
item.description: Michael Ramirez cartoon on oil prices and Iran strikes.

item.title: The administration’s radical idea: Retaining workers based on merit
item.description: A new rule from the Office of Personnel Management would discourage layoffs of the best performers.

item.title: Timothée Chalamet is wrong about opera
item.description: Post readers on opera and the language of war

item.title: Is there no coming back from this? I answered your questions.
item.description: David’s live chat with readers started at 12 p.m. ET on Monday. Read the transcript.

item.title: Don’t trust this $4 solution for getting a prescription
item.description: Should AI prescribe your meds? The evidence is lacking.

item.title: There are two winners in Iran. Neither one is America.
item.description: Oil disruption benefits Russia, as does less U.S. aid for Ukraine. And Iran distracts from China.

item.title: Congress is done waiting for Big Tech to protect kids online
item.description: Parents and their children deserve laws that work to reduce social media harms.

item.title: China may be preparing for nuclear war. Trump can’t ignore it.
item.description: Rather than aiming for nuclear deterrence, China appears to be building a stockpile to wage war.

item.title: Trump is trying something new in Iran. Hold on tight.
item.description: Critics demanding a “day after” plan are confusing this presidency with that of George W. Bush.

item.title: Your salted caramel mocha latte is destroying society
item.description: Edmund Burke would have had thoughts about the consumption of that vanilla sweet cream nitro cold brew.

item.title: Israeli officials are growing concerned
item.description: A bombing campaign nearing its military goals in Iran leaves the hardest questions unanswered.
